meta.title: DBX-CEA-1: System Prompts, RAG, Memory and MCP Tools

questions.stem: An agent must always respond in JSON with fields `answer` and `citations`. Where should that requirement be placed for the MOST consistent behavior?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instructions
Only in the final user message of each session
Inside retrieved document chunks
In the tool schema descriptions so retrieval can reference it

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because persistent formatting and behavioral constraints belong in the system-level instructions, which govern every turn of the conversation regardless of retrieved content or user phrasing.

**B** is incorrect because relying on the final user message means the constraint can be missing or inconsistently worded across sessions.

**C** is incorrect because retrieved chunks are variable and cannot reliably enforce output format on every response.

**D** is incorrect because tool schema descriptions inform the model what a tool does and when to call it; they are not persistent instruction surfaces that enforce output format across every conversational turn.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A prompt engineer notices that the system prompt contains several paragraphs of persona description that do not change agent outputs on any benchmark task. What is the BEST action?

options.text: Add even more persona detail to increase model engagement
Remove the decorative persona text and keep only instructions that materially change behavior
Move the persona paragraphs into every retrieved chunk
Convert the persona text to chain-of-thought scratchpad instructions

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because adding more decorative text wastes context tokens without improving task quality.

**B** is correct because context engineering focuses on maximizing useful signal in the context window; decorative persona text that does not affect outputs consumes budget without benefit.

**C** is incorrect because injecting persona text into retrieved chunks wastes retrieval context and conflates instructions with source material.

**D** is incorrect because chain-of-thought instructions are useful only when reasoning traces demonstrably improve output; converting irrelevant persona text to CoT still wastes tokens.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: An agent is given the instruction: "If the user asks about refunds, always escalate to a human agent." This instruction is not working reliably. Which change is MOST likely to improve compliance?

options.text: Place the escalation rule in a clearly delimited section at the top of the system prompt with explicit trigger conditions
Remove the instruction and rely on the model's pretraining to handle refund requests
Include the escalation rule only in the RAG retrieval results when the word "refund" appears
Move the escalation rule to the end of the system prompt where it gets more weight

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because explicit, clearly delimited rules with concrete trigger conditions at the top of the system prompt are processed most reliably. Burying rules in prose reduces compliance.

**B** is incorrect because pretrained models do not have organization-specific escalation policies and will not consistently apply them.

**C** is incorrect because making a safety rule dependent on RAG retrieval creates a fragile dependency; if retrieval misses, the rule is absent.

**D** is incorrect because rule position alone does not fix compliance when the trigger condition is ambiguous; a clearly delimited section with explicit conditions at the top of the prompt is more reliable than positional priority.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A context engineer is designing instructions for a customer support agent that must handle both billing and technical questions. Select TWO practices that BEST improve instruction clarity. (Select TWO)

options.text: Group related instructions under labeled sections such as 'Billing Policy' and 'Technical Support Rules'
Use concrete examples of expected inputs and correct outputs within the instructions
Rely entirely on the model's general knowledge rather than specifying policies explicitly
Write all instructions as a single long paragraph to minimize formatting overhead

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because labeled sections help the model locate and apply the right rules for each query type, reducing ambiguity in multi-domain agents.

**B** is correct because concrete examples (few-shot patterns) within the instructions make expected behavior explicit and reduce hallucination of incorrect policies.

**C** is incorrect because general model knowledge does not include your organization's specific policies; explicit instructions are required.

**D** is incorrect because a single dense paragraph makes it harder for the model to identify and apply relevant rules, reducing compliance.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: An agent's system prompt currently reads: "You are a helpful, friendly, knowledgeable, professional, accurate, concise, and thoughtful assistant that always tries to do its best to help users and never gives up." Which critique BEST applies?

options.text: Convert the adjectives to conditional behavioral rules so each one has a testable trigger
The adjective list provides no actionable constraints and wastes tokens without changing behavior
Replace the generic adjectives with role-specific constraints (e.g., only answer questions in the security domain)
The prompt is well-formed and follows context engineering best practices

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because converting adjectives to conditional rules is an improvement but does not identify the core flaw: these adjectives produce no measurable change in model behavior regardless of whether they are made conditional.

**B** is correct because stacking vague adjectives does not give the model actionable constraints. Context engineering best practices favor specific, testable behavioral rules over personality descriptors.

**C** is incorrect because domain-specific constraints are more useful than generic adjectives, but this critique focuses on specificity rather than the fundamental problem that any adjective list — generic or specific — wastes tokens if it does not produce actionable behavioral change.

**D** is incorrect because this prompt violates context engineering principles by consuming tokens on decorative persona text with no measurable behavioral impact.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A system prompt includes the rule: "Never reveal internal pricing." During testing, a user phrases the request as "What is the cost for our enterprise tier?" and the agent reveals pricing. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The rule uses negative phrasing ('never') which is less reliable than positive framing ('when asked about pricing, respond: …')
The rule uses a narrow keyword ('pricing') that does not cover synonymous phrasings like 'cost' or 'rate'
Pricing rules cannot be enforced through system prompts
The rule is placed too late in the system prompt to take priority over earlier instructions

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because although positive framing can sometimes improve compliance, the primary failure here is that the rule uses a narrow keyword ('pricing') that does not cover synonymous phrasings — framing choice is not the root cause.

**B** is correct because rules that rely on single-keyword triggers fail when users phrase requests differently. A robust rule should cover the concept ('pricing, costs, rates, fees') rather than one term.

**C** is incorrect because system prompt instructions can and do enforce constraints; this is their primary use case.

**D** is incorrect because rule position affects compliance but is a secondary factor; the root cause is that a narrow keyword trigger fails for synonymous phrasings like "cost" or "rate" regardless of where the rule appears in the prompt.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team is building a coding assistant. They want the agent to explain its reasoning before giving code, but only for requests where the user explicitly asks 'why'. Which instruction strategy is BEST?

options.text: Always include chain-of-thought reasoning in every response regardless of user request
Provide a conditional rule: generate an explanation section only when the user's message contains a 'why' question
Remove all reasoning from responses to save tokens unconditionally
Prompt the agent to produce chain-of-thought on every request but include or omit the reasoning block based on a flag in structured output

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because unconditional chain-of-thought adds tokens on every request, consuming budget when the user does not need it.

**B** is correct because conditional instructions tie context assembly to actual need, allowing the agent to spend tokens on reasoning only when it provides value to the user.

**C** is incorrect because removing all reasoning degrades output quality on questions that benefit from structured thinking.

**D** is incorrect because generating reasoning on every request still consumes tokens unconditionally; the flag controls output rendering, not whether the reasoning computation occurs, so this does not achieve the goal of reasoning only when the user asks 'why'.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team stores all company policies as a vector index. At query time, the top-3 relevant policy chunks are inserted into the system prompt. What is the PRIMARY risk of this design?

options.text: Retrieval failures or low similarity scores may cause critical policies to be omitted from the prompt
If retrieval latency is high, the top-3 policy chunks may not arrive before the model begins generating, causing inconsistent policy application
Using top-3 retrieval always returns too many tokens for any model
Using dynamic retrieval for policies means the same policy may be expressed differently across retrieval runs, causing inconsistent application

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because dynamically retrieved policies depend on retrieval quality. If the query is phrased unusually or the embedding similarity is low, the relevant policy may not appear in the top-3, leaving the agent without critical constraints.

**B** is incorrect because LLM APIs receive the full prompt before generating any output; retrieval latency affects when the request is dispatched, not mid-generation policy availability. The primary risk is retrieval quality failing to surface the policy, not a race condition with generation.

**C** is incorrect because whether top-3 is too many tokens depends on chunk size and is not universally true; this is not the primary risk.

**D** is incorrect because policies stored in a vector index are retrieved verbatim from source chunks; phrasing variability comes from the source documents, not from the retrieval mechanism. The primary risk is missing the policy entirely due to low similarity scores, not wording variation across runs.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team wants a managed semantic search index that automatically stays synchronized with a Delta table as rows are added or updated. Which Databricks feature BEST satisfies this requirement?

options.text: A self-managed FAISS index built from the Delta table on cluster startup
A Databricks Vector Search index with delta-sync enabled
A Databricks Online Table backed by the same Delta source
A direct-access Vector Search index updated by a nightly pipeline

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a self-managed FAISS index must be explicitly rebuilt whenever the Delta table changes; it does not automatically synchronize on row inserts or updates throughout the day.

**B** is correct because Databricks Vector Search with delta-sync keeps a vector index automatically synchronized with a source Delta table, supporting both incremental inserts and updates without manual re-indexing.

**C** is incorrect because Databricks Online Tables provide low-latency key-value lookups for structured data but do not support vector similarity search; they complement Vector Search rather than replacing it.

**D** is incorrect because a nightly pipeline update introduces up to a 24-hour lag between Delta table changes and index synchronization, which does not meet the requirement for automatic near-real-time sync as rows are added or updated.

[Databricks Vector Search documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/vector-search.html)

questions.stem: Search quality drops because each chunk contains many unrelated topics from long documents. Which change is MOST likely to improve retrieval precision?

options.text: Store every document as one very large chunk to maximize coverage
Disable embeddings and switch to row-level security filters only
Use a chunking strategy that preserves local semantic coherence, such as splitting at topic boundaries
Increase the SQL warehouse cluster size

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because large monolithic chunks embed multiple unrelated topics into a single vector, causing low-quality similarity matches.

**B** is incorrect because row-level security is an access-control mechanism, not a relevance signal. Disabling embeddings would eliminate semantic search entirely.

**C** is correct because chunking at semantic boundaries ensures each chunk's embedding represents one coherent topic, which dramatically improves retrieval precision and recall.

**D** is incorrect because compute cluster size affects query performance, not embedding quality or chunk relevance.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: A retrieval system needs to surface both semantically similar documents and documents with exact product code matches such as `SKU-40291`. Select TWO approaches that BEST address this requirement. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use hybrid retrieval that combines dense vector similarity with sparse keyword (BM25) signals
Apply a re-ranker model to re-score the merged candidate set before returning final results
Remove metadata fields from the index to simplify the schema
Raise the generation temperature to increase response variety

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because hybrid retrieval merges dense vector search (good for semantic similarity) with sparse BM25 (good for exact token matches like SKU codes), improving coverage for both query types.

**B** is correct because a re-ranker scores the merged candidate set using a more powerful cross-encoder, improving the final ordering before results are inserted into context.

**C** is incorrect because removing metadata fields reduces the index's filtering and scoring capabilities, making exact-match retrieval harder.

**D** is incorrect because generation temperature controls output randomness during text generation and has no effect on retrieval quality.

[Databricks Vector Search documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/vector-search.html)

questions.stem: A RAG agent is returning answers that cite irrelevant paragraphs. The retrieval logs show high similarity scores for the wrong chunks. What is the MOST likely root cause?

options.text: The embedding model is misaligned with the domain vocabulary; a domain-adapted embedding model would better capture relevance
The chunks are too large, causing relevant passages to be mixed with unrelated content and reducing cosine distance discrimination
The query rewriting step is expanding abbreviations in ways that change semantic meaning before embedding
The Unity Catalog permissions are too restrictive for the embedding service

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because high similarity scores for wrong chunks indicate the embedding space is not aligned with domain-specific meaning. Specialized or fine-tuned embedding models better represent domain vocabulary and improve retrieval precision.

**B** is incorrect because oversized chunks can reduce retrieval precision, but they typically produce uniformly low similarity scores for the right content rather than high scores for wrong chunks; high false-positive scores point more specifically to embedding space misalignment with the domain vocabulary.

**C** is incorrect because query preprocessing errors would shift similarity scores but would not systematically produce high scores for irrelevant chunks; the symptom of high scores for wrong documents is a stronger signal of embedding model misalignment than of query rewriting errors.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog permissions control access, not similarity ranking.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: A team builds a RAG pipeline that retrieves the top-20 chunks and passes all of them to the model. Users report slow responses and sometimes incoherent answers. Which improvement is MOST targeted?

options.text: Reduce top-k to a smaller set of the most relevant chunks and apply a re-ranker to ensure quality
Apply a reranker after retrieving top-20 to keep only the 5 most relevant chunks before passing to the model
Switch to a SQL-only retrieval strategy with no embeddings
Increase chunk overlap from 50 to 200 tokens to ensure boundary continuity reduces context fragmentation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because passing 20 chunks floods the context window with low-relevance material, increasing latency and diluting signal. Reducing top-k with re-ranking provides fewer, higher-quality chunks.

**B** is incorrect because applying a reranker to the top-20 still incurs the full retrieval cost for 20 items and adds reranking overhead without reducing the initial candidate set; combining a smaller top-k with reranking as in option A addresses both retrieval efficiency and context quality more directly.

**C** is incorrect because SQL retrieval retrieves structured rows, not unstructured document chunks, and does not solve the chunk-volume problem.

**D** is incorrect because larger chunk overlap increases the size of each chunk and the total tokens injected into context, which would worsen both the latency and incoherence problems rather than improve them.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: A retrieval pipeline embeds document chunks using a general-purpose embedding model but user queries are highly technical and use domain acronyms not common in general web text. What change would MOST improve retrieval quality?

options.text: Re-embed the corpus using a domain-adapted embedding model trained on similar technical content
Increase the chunk overlap window from 50 to 100 tokens
Apply query expansion to rephrase technical acronyms into their full forms before embedding
Increase chunk size to 1,000 tokens so more context surrounds each technical term when embedded

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because general-purpose embedding models underrepresent domain-specific acronyms and terminology. A domain-adapted model produces embeddings that better align query and document representations for specialized vocabulary.

**B** is incorrect because chunk overlap affects boundary continuity, not the quality of embedding representation for domain terms.

**C** is incorrect because query expansion can improve recall when the corpus uses full terms but queries use abbreviations, but it does not fix the root cause: the embedding model is not trained on this domain's vocabulary. Re-embedding with a domain-adapted model addresses the misalignment at the source.

**D** is incorrect because larger chunks embed more surrounding context per vector, which can dilute the signal from specific technical terms rather than improve their representation; the root issue is that the embedding model does not understand the domain acronyms regardless of how much surrounding text is included.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: A Vector Search query filters by `department = 'finance'` before performing semantic search. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this approach?

options.text: Pre-filtering restricts the candidate pool to relevant documents, reducing noise in retrieved results and improving precision
Pre-filtering replaces the need for embeddings entirely
Pre-filtering increases the total number of results returned
Pre-filtering eliminates latency because no vector comparison is performed

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because metadata pre-filtering scopes the similarity search to a relevant subset, preventing cross-department documents from occupying top-k slots and improving both precision and context quality.

**B** is incorrect because pre-filtering narrows the search space but does not replace the semantic similarity computation over the filtered set.

**C** is incorrect because pre-filtering constrains the candidate pool; it typically reduces, not increases, the number of potential results.

**D** is incorrect because vector comparison is still performed over the filtered subset; latency is reduced but not eliminated.

[Databricks Vector Search documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/vector-search.html)

questions.stem: A support agent must remember user-stated preferences such as preferred language and communication style across sessions that occur days apart. Which memory design is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Keep all historical conversation turns in the active prompt for every new session
Persist a structured user profile in an external store such as Lakebase and retrieve it at session start
Reconstruct preferences from the current session transcript at the start of each session
Cache the preference in the model's context window by prepending it to every message in the current session

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because keeping all historical turns in the prompt grows unboundedly and becomes impractical after many sessions.

**B** is correct because cross-session preferences require durable external persistence. A structured user profile stored in Lakebase or another persistent store can be fetched at session start and injected into the context selectively.

**C** is incorrect because reconstructing preferences from the current session transcript only captures what was expressed in the present session; preferences stated in sessions days apart are not available in the current transcript and would be permanently lost.

**D** is incorrect because the model's context window is session-scoped and cleared between sessions; prepending preferences to every message in a single session does not persist that information when the user returns days later.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: An agent's conversation history is growing so large that it causes context overflow. The agent still needs to act on decisions made in earlier turns. Which technique BEST addresses this?

options.text: Delete all history after every message to keep the context short
Summarize older turns into a compact state representation and retain only recent verbatim exchanges
Duplicate the full transcript in both the system prompt and the user message
Move the entire conversation history into Unity Catalog column comments

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because deleting all history loses prior decisions the agent needs for coherent multi-turn behavior.

**B** is correct because summarization compresses older turns into a dense state description, preserving decision-relevant information while dramatically reducing token usage.

**C** is incorrect because duplicating the transcript worsens the overflow problem by consuming more tokens.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog column comments are metadata for data assets, not a conversational memory mechanism.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to track agent reasoning state across a long multi-step workflow where the agent calls several tools in sequence. Which approach BEST supports durable state between tool calls?

options.text: Store intermediate results and reasoning summaries in an external key-value or document store and pass only the current step's context to the model
Include the full output of every previous tool call in the next model invocation
Pass only the most recent tool output to the model and rely on conversational repair if the agent needs earlier results
Maintain a full tool-output log in the prompt and rely on the model to select what is relevant

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because storing intermediate state externally and injecting only the relevant summary into each model call keeps the context lean while preserving workflow continuity across many steps.

**B** is incorrect because including full tool outputs from every prior step grows the context linearly with workflow length, eventually causing overflow.

**C** is incorrect because passing only the most recent output risks the agent losing track of earlier steps in the workflow; multi-step workflows often require context from several prior tool calls, not just the most recent one.

**D** is incorrect because including all tool outputs in the prompt grows context linearly with workflow length, eventually causing overflow — the same fundamental problem as including the full output of every previous tool call described in option B.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A financial advisory agent must distinguish between facts the user stated in the current session versus facts learned in previous sessions. Which design supports this distinction MOST reliably?

options.text: Store all session facts inline in the chat history without any labeling or tagging
Maintain separate memory scopes: a labeled in-session scratchpad for current-turn facts and a labeled long-term profile for cross-session facts
Use a single flat memory block and rely on the model to infer fact provenance
Retrieve the full user history on every query regardless of relevance to avoid missing any detail

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because unlabeled inline facts give the model no signal about recency or source, making it harder to apply appropriate confidence or staleness checks.

**B** is correct because distinct labeled memory scopes let the model and the engineer apply different confidence levels, update frequencies, and staleness logic to in-session versus long-term facts.

**C** is incorrect because a flat block without scope labels requires the model to infer provenance, which it cannot do reliably.

**D** is incorrect because retrieving all history on every query wastes context tokens and may surface stale or irrelevant information.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team is designing an agent that must log conversation state for compliance and also support fast in-session recall. Select TWO storage choices that BEST serve these dual needs. (Select TWO)

options.text: Write structured conversation state to a Delta table for durable, auditable storage
Use a low-latency key-value cache such as Redis for fast in-session state reads
Write conversation state to a shared Redis cache for fast cross-session retrieval
Log conversation state to a Unity Catalog volume as JSON files for compliance

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Delta tables provide ACID transactions, time-travel, and audit history, satisfying compliance requirements for durable conversation logs.

**B** is correct because a low-latency cache provides sub-millisecond reads of recent session state, keeping agent response times fast without re-querying the Delta table on every turn.

**C** is incorrect because Redis provides fast in-session reads but is not designed for durable compliance logging; it lacks ACID transactions, time-travel, and audit history, making it unsuitable as a compliance store.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog volumes support file storage but lack the low-latency key-value access needed for fast in-session state reads; reading files on every turn requires full file parsing rather than sub-millisecond lookup.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: MLflow is used to track an agent's experiment runs across a multi-step evaluation workflow. What is the PRIMARY benefit of using MLflow for this purpose?

options.text: MLflow provides reproducible run tracking with logged parameters, metrics, and artifacts that allow state to be inspected and replayed
MLflow automatically summarizes conversation history into compact prompts
MLflow stores embeddings in a vector index for semantic retrieval of past runs
MLflow generates the system prompt automatically from past run metadata

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because MLflow's run tracking records parameters, metrics, and artifacts with timestamps, enabling inspection, debugging, and reproducibility of agent behavior across steps.

**B** is incorrect because MLflow does not automatically summarize conversation history; it stores what is explicitly logged by the application.

**C** is incorrect because MLflow is a model lifecycle platform; vector storage and retrieval are handled by Databricks Vector Search.

**D** is incorrect because MLflow does not generate system prompts; prompt management is the engineer's responsibility.

[MLflow documentation](https://mlflow.org/docs/latest/index.html)

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY value of defining a well-structured tool schema (name, description, parameters with types) for an agent?

options.text: It guarantees the model will never produce incorrect output when the tool is used
It allows the model to understand the tool's purpose and call signature so it can invoke the tool correctly
It replaces authentication and access-control mechanisms for the tool
It eliminates the need for system prompt instructions that mention the tool

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a schema improves reliability but does not guarantee correct outputs; the model can still reason incorrectly about when or how to use the tool.

**B** is correct because a clear schema with names, descriptions, and typed parameters gives the model the information it needs to decide when and how to call the tool correctly.

**C** is incorrect because authentication and governance are infrastructure concerns separate from the schema definition.

**D** is incorrect because system prompt instructions about when and why to use tools complement the schema and should not be eliminated.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A governed analytics agent must query sensitive sales tables. The security team requires that all queries be subject to row-level security and column masking. Which design BEST meets this requirement?

options.text: Grant the agent access through a governed SQL endpoint using parameterized query templates but without row-level security enforcement
Embed database credentials directly in the system prompt for the model's convenience
Expose curated Unity Catalog functions or governed SQL tools that enforce row-level security and masking policies
Create dedicated views per sensitivity level and grant the agent access only to the appropriate view

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because parameterized query templates prevent SQL injection but do not enforce row-level security; without RLS, the agent can still retrieve rows from sensitivity levels it should not access, failing the security requirement.

**B** is incorrect because embedding credentials in the system prompt creates a significant security risk; credentials should be managed by secrets management systems.

**C** is correct because Unity Catalog-backed functions and governed SQL tools automatically enforce row-level security, column masking, and audit logging on every query the agent makes.

**D** is incorrect because view-based access controls restrict row access but do not apply column masking policies to redact specific fields within permitted rows; Unity Catalog's built-in RLS and masking policies as in option C provide more comprehensive enforcement.

[Databricks Unity Catalog documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: A team is integrating external REST APIs as agent tools. Select TWO practices that BEST improve reliability and debuggability. (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool call inputs against the declared parameter schema before sending the API request
Return structured error responses with actionable messages so the agent can retry or ask for clarification
Suppress all tool call logs to minimize storage overhead
Accept all inputs as a single untyped free-text string to simplify the schema

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because pre-call schema validation prevents malformed requests from reaching the API, reducing errors and wasted latency.

**B** is correct because structured error responses give the model enough context to recover, retry with corrected parameters, or surface a meaningful message to the user.

**C** is incorrect because suppressing logs removes the observability needed to diagnose failures in production.

**D** is incorrect because a single free-text string provides no type safety or schema guidance, making it harder for the model to construct correct calls.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: An agent using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) needs to expose a company's internal knowledge base as a resource server. What does MCP's resource abstraction primarily allow?

options.text: MCP resources allow a server to expose structured or unstructured content that a host agent can read into context without embedding retrieval logic in the agent itself
MCP resources replace the need for a vector index by storing all documents inside the protocol layer
MCP resources are exclusively used for streaming audio and video media content
MCP resources automatically summarize and compress documents before returning them to the agent

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because MCP's resource abstraction lets a server expose any content (files, database rows, API responses) as named resources that a host can fetch and inject into context, decoupling retrieval implementation from agent logic.

**B** is incorrect because MCP is a protocol for exposing capabilities; the underlying storage and retrieval (e.g., a vector index) still exists on the server side.

**C** is incorrect because MCP resources are a general-purpose abstraction for any content type, not specifically for media streams.

**D** is incorrect because MCP resources deliver content as-is; summarization would need to be implemented explicitly on the server.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: An MCP server exposes a tool called `run_sql` that executes arbitrary SQL on a data warehouse. A security reviewer flags this as high risk. What is the BEST mitigation?

options.text: Restrict the tool to a predefined set of parameterized query templates and enforce Unity Catalog permissions on the service account
Wrap run_sql with an allowlist that permits only SELECT statements and blocks all DDL and DML
Require the service account to authenticate per-request using short-lived OAuth tokens rather than a long-lived credential
Move the tool from MCP to a direct function call to improve execution performance

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because parameterized query templates prevent SQL injection by separating code from data, while Unity Catalog permissions limit the blast radius of any misuse to only the tables the service account is authorized to access.

**B** is incorrect because restricting to SELECT statements reduces the blast radius but does not prevent data exfiltration at scale; Unity Catalog permissions are still needed to scope which tables the service account can access, and parameterized templates are needed to prevent injection within SELECT statements.

**C** is incorrect because short-lived credentials reduce the risk of credential theft but do not prevent a compromised agent from running arbitrary SQL within its permitted scope; parameterized templates and table-level permissions are needed to constrain what can be executed.

**D** is incorrect because changing the invocation mechanism (MCP vs. direct call) does not address the security risk of arbitrary SQL execution.

[Databricks Unity Catalog documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: An agent has access to 12 tools. Users report that it frequently chooses the wrong tool for a task. What is the MOST effective prompt-level fix?

options.text: Improve each tool's description to clearly state its purpose, expected inputs, and the situations where it should be preferred over similar tools
Dynamically trim the active tool set to only the 3-4 most likely tools based on query intent before passing to the model
Reduce the number of tools to 1 to eliminate routing errors entirely
Add a tool-routing preamble step where a lightweight classifier assigns the query to a tool category before the main agent runs

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the model selects tools based on their name and description in the schema. Clearer, more distinctive descriptions that highlight when each tool should be preferred reduce selection errors without changing the tool set.

**B** is incorrect because dynamically trimming the tool set can help with routing accuracy but adds a pre-classification step before every call and may drop tools the model legitimately needs; improving tool descriptions is a more targeted prompt-level fix that does not require additional infrastructure.

**C** is incorrect because reducing functionality to fix routing errors is not a viable solution for a multi-capability agent.

**D** is incorrect because a pre-routing classifier adds latency and architectural complexity and does not address the root cause that tool descriptions are ambiguous; a classifier would face the same disambiguation problem if descriptions are unclear.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: An agent uses a tool that writes records to a production database. During a demonstration, the agent incorrectly calls this tool with test data. Which safeguard would have BEST prevented this?

options.text: Require explicit human confirmation before any tool call that performs a write or delete operation
Grant the agent read-only database access so all write calls automatically fail with an error
Use environment variables to distinguish demo from production at invocation time and route write calls to a staging database during testing
Implement transaction staging: write to a staging record first, then confirm the commit only after a validation step

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a human-in-the-loop confirmation gate for write and delete operations ensures that potentially irreversible side effects are approved by a person before execution, which is the standard safeguard for destructive tool calls.

**B** is incorrect because read-only access would prevent the agent from performing legitimate writes as well, removing the feature entirely rather than adding a safeguard.

**C** is incorrect because environment-variable routing could redirect writes during demos but requires the demo configuration to be correctly set each time, creating a setup dependency that is easy to miss; requiring explicit human confirmation before write operations is a more robust safeguard.

**D** is incorrect because transaction staging reduces the impact of incorrect writes and allows rollback, but it still permits the write to begin without human review; the safeguard in option A prevents the write from starting until a person explicitly approves it.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes what must be managed within a model's context window in a production agent?

options.text: Only the system prompt and retrieved chunks count toward input tokens; conversation history is compressed automatically
The system prompt, conversation history, retrieved chunks, tool call inputs and outputs, and the current user message all consume context tokens
Context limits apply only to input tokens; output token costs are billed separately and do not contribute to the context window
SQL warehouse result caches eliminate context window constraints for production agents

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because all components injected into the model's input — system prompt, history, retrieved chunks, tool traces, and the user message — count toward the context window; conversation history is not compressed automatically by the model API.

**B** is correct because every element passed to the model — system prompt, history, retrieved context, tool traces, and the new user message — competes for the same finite context window budget.

**C** is incorrect because the context window encompasses the full sequence including both the input prompt and generated output; treating input and output as entirely independent limits leads to underestimating how quickly the context window fills during multi-turn conversations with long responses.

**D** is incorrect because result caches store query outputs and have no effect on model context limits.

[Databricks documentation on large language models](https://docs.databricks.com/large-language-models/index.html)

questions.stem: An agent's context window overflows after 8 turns because tool call traces from prior steps are never pruned. Which strategy MOST directly solves this?

options.text: Prune or summarize completed tool traces after the agent has processed their results, retaining only the key outcome
Limit the model's max_tokens to force shorter per-turn outputs, reducing total trace size over time
Store tool traces in a Unity Catalog table so they are not in the prompt
Upgrade to an extended context model tier (200k tokens) so traces never need pruning

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because completed tool traces whose results have already been incorporated can be compressed to just their outcome (e.g., 'searched for X, found Y'), dramatically reducing token accumulation across long workflows.

**B** is incorrect because limiting max_tokens constrains current output length but does not remove the accumulated traces of completed tool calls already in the input prompt; the overflow comes from historic traces in the context, not from the current response length.

**C** is incorrect because storing traces externally still requires fetching and injecting them into context if needed; the token cost is not reduced by their storage location alone.

**D** is incorrect because upgrading to a larger context model defers but does not eliminate the overflow problem for workflows that grow unboundedly; traces must still be pruned or summarized to keep the prompt efficient regardless of which context tier is used.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to allocate context tokens across system instructions, retrieved chunks, and conversation history. Which approach BEST prevents any one component from crowding out the others?

options.text: Define explicit maximum token budgets for each context section and enforce them programmatically before model invocation
Use the largest possible model to avoid worrying about token limits
Use prompt compression techniques to auto-summarize each section down to a target budget before assembly
Combine all sections into one undelimited block so the model can self-organize the content

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because explicit token budgets per section give the engineer deterministic control over what enters the context window, preventing retrieval or history from crowding out critical instructions.

**B** is incorrect because using a larger model raises costs and still requires good context management; even large models have finite context windows.

**C** is incorrect because auto-summarization compresses content but does not give the engineer deterministic control over the final token budget; explicit per-section token limits enforce bounds programmatically, whereas auto-summarization output length can vary and may still cause overflow.

**D** is incorrect because an undelimited block makes it harder for the model to distinguish section types and easier for one section to overflow into another.

[Databricks documentation on large language models](https://docs.databricks.com/large-language-models/index.html)

questions.stem: A long-running research agent accumulates 80,000 tokens of context after 20 steps. The model's limit is 32,000 tokens. Which compaction approach preserves the most decision-relevant information?

options.text: Truncate the oldest tokens blindly until the total fits within the limit
Use a structured compaction step that summarizes completed reasoning phases, retains open goals and key findings, and discards redundant intermediate steps
Delete the entire conversation and ask the user to restart from scratch
Upgrade to a model with a 128k context limit and make no other changes

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because blind truncation removes the oldest tokens, which often include the original task specification and key early decisions that are still relevant.

**B** is correct because structured compaction intelligently compresses completed phases into summaries while preserving open goals, key findings, and decisions that remain actionable, resulting in a much more useful compressed context than blind truncation.

**C** is incorrect because restarting discards all accumulated work and creates a poor user experience.

**D** is incorrect because while a larger context model helps, relying solely on model upgrades does not solve the architectural problem for workflows that grow beyond any fixed limit.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: A team is assembling context for a customer service agent. Select TWO techniques that BEST help stay within the context budget while preserving answer quality. (Select TWO)

options.text: Retrieve fewer but higher-quality chunks using a re-ranker rather than always passing the top-20 results
Summarize conversation history older than the last 3 turns into a compact state block
Include all available policy documents in every prompt regardless of their relevance to the current query
Increase the system prompt length to cover every possible edge case in a single static document

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a re-ranker selects the highest-quality subset of retrieved candidates, reducing token usage while improving signal-to-noise in retrieved context.

**B** is correct because summarizing older history compresses verbose conversation logs into a compact representation, freeing tokens for fresh retrieved context and the current turn.

**C** is incorrect because including all policy documents regardless of relevance floods the context with noise, reducing both quality and staying within the token budget.

**D** is incorrect because a longer system prompt consumes more fixed tokens on every request, worsening the budget problem across all queries.

[Databricks documentation on large language models](https://docs.databricks.com/large-language-models/index.html)

questions.stem: A team observes that their agent's response quality degrades when the retrieved context section exceeds 6,000 tokens even though the model supports 100k tokens total. What phenomenon BEST explains this?

options.text: Models have difficulty attending to information buried in the middle of a very long context, sometimes called the 'lost in the middle' effect
The model tends to prioritize instructions that appear in the middle of the context window, so 6,000 tokens pushes content into the highest-attention zone
Token-per-second throughput decreases as payload size grows, causing the observed latency increase that engineers mistake for quality degradation
Longer retrieved contexts force the model to use a smaller output vocabulary

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because research shows that transformer models tend to attend more reliably to information at the beginning and end of a long context, with material in the middle receiving less attention — this degrades answer quality when critical context is buried in the middle of a large retrieval block.

**B** is incorrect because research shows models attend LESS reliably to content in the middle of a long context window, not more; describing this as a 'prioritized' zone inverts the actual 'lost in the middle' phenomenon that explains the quality degradation.

**C** is incorrect because throughput reduction explains latency increases, not answer quality degradation; the question specifically describes quality dropping rather than responses slowing down, pointing to an attention distribution problem rather than a compute throughput constraint.

**D** is incorrect because vocabulary size is fixed by the tokenizer and does not change based on context length.

[Databricks RAG documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/retrieval-augmented-generation.html)

questions.stem: Which of the following is the MOST direct consequence of exhausting a model's context window during a multi-turn conversation?

options.text: The agent framework automatically segments the conversation into multiple parallel calls and merges the results
Older content must be dropped or the request will fail, potentially losing important context
The model applies sliding-window attention that gracefully handles overflow by summarizing dropped tokens before they leave the window
The SQL warehouse increases its memory allocation to compensate for the overflow

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because most LLM frameworks do not automatically segment long conversations into parallel calls; context overflow handling must be implemented explicitly by the engineer, and without that implementation the API call fails when the limit is exceeded.

**B** is correct because the context window is a hard limit. When exceeded, the application must either truncate or summarize older content, or the API call will fail with a token limit error — either outcome risks losing important conversational context.

**C** is incorrect because while some model architectures use sliding-window attention during training, production API calls do not gracefully summarize dropped tokens at inference time; if the input exceeds the context limit, the API returns a token limit error rather than silently truncating and summarizing.

**D** is incorrect because SQL warehouse memory and model context windows are independent systems with no connection.

[Databricks documentation on large language models](https://docs.databricks.com/large-language-models/index.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to compare two RAG configurations — one with top-5 retrieval and one with top-10 retrieval — using a fixed set of 200 evaluation questions. Which evaluation approach is MOST rigorous?

options.text: Run both configurations against all 200 questions, compute answer quality metrics for each, and perform a statistical comparison
Ask a single engineer to review 5 example outputs from each configuration and pick a winner
Deploy the preferred configuration to production immediately and monitor user satisfaction scores
Evaluate only response latency because answer quality is too subjective to measure objectively

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because running both configurations against the same benchmark and comparing metrics provides statistically meaningful, reproducible evidence for configuration decisions.

**B** is incorrect because a single engineer reviewing 5 examples is not statistically valid and introduces personal bias.

**C** is incorrect because deploying untested changes to production exposes all users to potential quality regressions before the configuration is validated.

**D** is incorrect because answer quality can be measured using metrics such as faithfulness, groundedness, and answer relevance; dismissing quality measurement is a false constraint.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)

questions.stem: A governance review is focused on preventing sensitive PII from entering agent prompts. Select TWO controls that BEST align with this goal. (Select TWO)

options.text: Classify sensitive data sources in Unity Catalog and restrict retrieval tool access by classification tag
Apply column masking policies so PII fields are redacted before being returned to the agent
Increase top-k retrieval to maximize coverage and reduce the chance of missing relevant records
Require all agent responses to include a data privacy disclaimer footer

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because classifying data sources in Unity Catalog and enforcing tag-based access controls prevents the retrieval tool from fetching sensitive documents in the first place.

**B** is correct because column masking policies redact PII at the data layer before results reach the agent, ensuring sensitive values never enter the context window.

**C** is incorrect because increasing top-k widens retrieval and is more likely to surface sensitive records, not less.

**D** is incorrect because response disclaimers do not prevent PII from entering the prompt; they only affect what is shown to the user after the fact.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team evaluates their RAG agent only on final answer quality (correctness) and ignores retrieval diagnostics. What key operational risk does this create?

options.text: The team will automatically reduce storage costs by skipping retrieval logs
The team cannot distinguish whether answer failures are caused by retrieval gaps or model generation errors, making targeted fixes impossible
The team will achieve better tool routing performance as a side effect
The team can eliminate the need for any offline evaluation entirely

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because evaluation scope has no direct effect on storage costs; retrieval logs are written regardless of whether they are analyzed.

**B** is correct because without retrieval diagnostics (precision, recall, chunk relevance scores), a team cannot tell whether low answer quality stems from retrieval returning the wrong chunks or from the model generating poor text from good chunks. Without this distinction, fixes are guesswork.

**C** is incorrect because tool routing is unrelated to answer-only evaluation; measuring it requires separate tool-selection diagnostics.

**D** is incorrect because ignoring retrieval diagnostics reduces evaluation coverage rather than replacing offline evaluation.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)

questions.stem: Unity Catalog is used to register agent tools and track which version of a tool an agent used to produce a specific answer. What governance capability does this primarily enable?

options.text: Lineage tracking: the ability to trace an agent's answer back to the exact tool version and data assets that contributed to it
Automatic answer correction when a newer tool version is registered in the catalog
Reduction in model temperature requirements for registered tools
Elimination of the need for vector search indexes when tools are cataloged

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Unity Catalog provides data and asset lineage, allowing teams to trace which tool version, data table, or model was used to produce a given output — essential for audits, debugging, and compliance.

**B** is incorrect because updating a tool version in the catalog does not retroactively fix or recompute past answers.

**C** is incorrect because Unity Catalog is a governance layer and has no effect on model generation parameters like temperature.

**D** is incorrect because Unity Catalog and vector search serve complementary roles; registering tools in the catalog does not remove the need for retrieval indexes.

[Databricks Unity Catalog documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/data-governance/unity-catalog/index.html)

questions.stem: An agent is deployed to production and the team wants to detect prompt injection attacks where adversarial text in retrieved documents attempts to override system instructions. Which defense is MOST effective?

options.text: Clearly delimit retrieved content from instructions in the prompt and add input validation that flags or strips instruction-override patterns in retrieved text
Increase the model temperature to randomize behavior and reduce attack predictability
Store all documents in a SQL table and never embed them in the prompt to avoid retrieval entirely
Require users to manually approve every retrieved chunk before it is inserted into context

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because structural prompt delimiting (e.g., XML tags or explicit section markers) helps the model distinguish instructions from data, and input validation to detect and strip injection patterns in retrieved text provides a pre-insertion defense layer.

**B** is incorrect because higher temperature increases output randomness, which does not prevent the model from following injected instructions and may introduce other quality issues.

**C** is incorrect because storing documents in SQL without embedding them defeats the purpose of RAG; context injection is required for the model to use the documents.

**D** is incorrect because requiring user approval of every chunk creates extreme friction and is not practical at production scale.

[Build agents on Databricks](https://docs.databricks.com/en/generative-ai/agent-framework/build-agents.html)

questions.stem: A team uses Databricks Agent Evaluation to assess their RAG agent. The evaluation framework reports low 'groundedness' scores. What does this indicate?

options.text: The agent's answers frequently contain claims not supported by the retrieved context, indicating hallucination
The retrieval step is returning too few results for each query
The model's generation temperature is set too low
The system prompt is missing a required greeting instruction

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because groundedness measures whether the agent's answer is supported by the retrieved context. A low groundedness score means the model is making claims that cannot be traced back to the retrieved documents — a hallucination signal.

**B** is incorrect because retrieval volume (top-k) is a separate metric; groundedness measures answer-to-context fidelity, not retrieval breadth.

**C** is incorrect because temperature affects output randomness, not whether answers are grounded in retrieved context.

**D** is incorrect because a missing greeting instruction is a formatting issue and would not produce low groundedness scores.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)

questions.stem: A team logs every agent request and response to a Delta table for monitoring. After a week, they notice the agent returns different answers to the same question on different days. Which root cause should they investigate FIRST?

options.text: Changes in the retrieval index content or model version between days, which the logged request metadata should reveal
The model version was silently rolled back to a previous checkpoint, producing different generation outputs for identical inputs
The SQL warehouse cluster is throttling requests on high-traffic days
An A/B testing configuration is inadvertently routing some requests to a different prompt template

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because non-deterministic answers over time are most commonly explained by changes in retrieved context (index updates) or model version changes. The logged metadata for each request should include the retrieval results and model version, enabling root cause isolation.

**B** is incorrect because a model version rollback is a valid investigation lead, but it is already captured within what option A describes; logged request metadata typically includes the model version, making version changes visible in the same metadata review that reveals index content changes.

**C** is incorrect because warehouse throttling affects latency, not the content of model-generated answers.

**D** is incorrect because A/B routing to different prompt templates is a valid operational cause of answer variation, but it is less commonly the default explanation than index content or model version changes; the question asks what to investigate FIRST, and A covers the causes most directly visible in standard request metadata.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)

questions.stem: A compliance team requires that all agent answers about regulatory topics can be traced to an approved source document. Select TWO implementation choices that BEST satisfy this requirement. (Select TWO)

options.text: Include source document identifiers in the retrieved chunks and require the agent to cite them in its response
Log the retrieved chunks and their source metadata alongside each agent response in a Delta table
Increase the model temperature to produce more varied and creative citations
Allow the model to generate citations from its pretrained knowledge without retrieval when no document matches

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because including source identifiers in retrieved chunks and instructing the agent to cite them links each answer claim to a specific approved document, satisfying the traceability requirement at the response level.

**B** is correct because logging retrieved chunks and their metadata (document ID, version, retrieval timestamp) alongside each response creates an auditable record that compliance reviewers can inspect.

**C** is incorrect because higher temperature introduces more randomness into citation generation, which would undermine traceability and could produce hallucinated citations.

**D** is incorrect because pretrained model knowledge cannot be traced to specific approved documents; retrieval-backed citations are required for compliance.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)

questions.stem: A team wants to continuously monitor a deployed RAG agent for answer quality degradation as the retrieval index is updated weekly. Which approach BEST enables this?

options.text: Run a fixed evaluation benchmark against the agent after each index update and track quality metrics over time in MLflow
Manually review 10 randomly selected answers each week and make a subjective judgment call
Block all index updates until a dedicated QA team individually approves each new document
Monitor only API error rates as a proxy for overall answer quality

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because running a consistent evaluation benchmark after each index change and tracking metrics in MLflow creates a time-series view of quality, enabling automated alerts when metrics degrade beyond a defined threshold.

**B** is incorrect because 10 random answers provide insufficient statistical power to detect subtle degradation, and the process is too manual to be operationally sustainable at scale.

**C** is incorrect because blocking all index updates creates operational bottlenecks and does not scale with frequent content changes.

**D** is incorrect because API error rates measure service availability, not answer quality; an agent can have 100% uptime and still produce degraded answers.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)

questions.stem: A context engineer is asked to define an acceptance threshold for deploying a new agent version. The current production agent achieves 82% groundedness and 78% answer relevance on the evaluation set. What is the MOST principled approach to setting the deployment threshold?

options.text: Set the deployment threshold at the current production metrics and require the new version to meet or exceed them on the same evaluation set before deploying
Set the threshold at 100% for all metrics to ensure the new version is perfect before any deployment
Skip thresholds entirely and deploy the new version if at least one metric improves over the baseline
Use latency as the only deployment threshold because correctness metrics are considered too noisy to trust

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because using current production performance as the baseline threshold ensures new versions do not regress on the metrics that reflect user value. The new version must match or exceed the baseline to be deployed, balancing rigor with practicality.

**B** is incorrect because 100% thresholds are unachievable for probabilistic models and would permanently block all deployments.

**C** is incorrect because accepting a new version when only one metric improves allows regressions on other important quality dimensions, which could harm users.

**D** is incorrect because latency alone does not capture answer quality; groundedness and relevance are equally important deployment signals for a RAG agent.

[Databricks Agent Evaluation documentation](https://docs.databricks.com/generative-ai/agent-evaluation/index.html)
